1	# Interview — 2026-04-02
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Tenth interview. Same day as the previous (2026-04-02T030115), which covered the first
6	guild-native inscription cycle and vocabulary fatigue. Today Sean has completed the first
7	full cohort review: 8 commissions analyzed, quality analysis artifact written, commission
8	log fully annotated. He's been working all day — Clerk review, ID normalization work,
9	scorer bug fixes, patron review of normalize-IDs, quality analysis, fixup spec drafted.
10	The pipeline is now described as stable.
11	
12	## Key Quotes
13	
14	**"Just noting that it felt like most commissions needed revisions and/or missed the mark.
15	Turned out to be 50%, so arguably not 'most' but still a lot."**
16	— On what prompted the quality question. Perception read higher than reality; partial
17	outcomes land heavier than successes.
18	
19	**"Partial outcomes land heavier, for sure. There's the work of figuring out the gaps and
20	drafting+submitting a whole new commission. Plus, I suspect, that 'cruft' and tech debt
21	will accumulate at a faster rate when agents have to repeatedly iterate to achieve a goal."**
22	— The weight of a partial isn't just effort, it's a forward-looking concern about what
23	iteration leaves in the codebase. The tech debt suspicion is notable: more iterations =
24	more accumulated mess, not just more time.
25	
26	**"That was just Coco doing it because I didn't want to think about the details here, tbh.
27	I really want to be able to go the other way — 'mountains, not trails' — and let the
28	system figure out the exhaustive details."**
29	— On the exhaustive fixup spec. Coco wrote it; it wasn't Sean changing approach. The
30	quality analysis prescription (be exhaustive) is in direct tension with the design
31	philosophy (point at the mountain).
32	
33	**"They are in conflict right now. But I hope the exhaustive specs are a temporary
34	workaround, and we can build in compensating features — curricula for one, yes, but I
35	honestly think baked in planning and review cycles will be the key."**
36	— Most direct statement yet of the structural fix. Not "better spec writing" but pipeline
37	architecture: planning and review as built-in steps.
38	
39	**"I did ponder how much money, energy, and water is being wasted around the globe as
40	other people doing similar work encounter that same failure mode."**
41	— On the abandoned commission (no commit instruction). Systems-level framing of a
42	workflow failure. Notable: he immediately reframed the abandoned outcome as preferable to
43	bad code landing in the codebase.
44	
45	**"Morally, I'm honestly still on the fence about AI... the environmental, social, and
46	personal costs to its adoption are real. And yet here I sit, at the middle of a bold
47	experiment in AI and using it at a rate far greater than I ever had previously."**
48	— Background concern, not daily friction. But clearly present.
49	
50	**"I don't think I would expect human users of this system, myself included, to ponder
51	that level of minor implication from how things are worded."**
52	— On the normalize-IDs spec framing trap ("make everything work like Clerk" let the agent
53	skip Clerk). Clean articulation of the ceiling on patron craft: some failure modes can't
54	be reliably caught at spec-writing time.
55	
56	**"It's been internally consistent, and tracking my own assessments pretty closely. It's
57	probably caught more things than I have, to be honest."**
58	— On the quality scorer. Calibrated, trustworthy, slightly raises the floor.
59	
60	**"I think there will be a pretty clean shift from 'have Coco implement things
61	interactively' to 'dispatch, collect experiment data via pipeline, and have Coco
62	review+suggest followups'."**
63	— A forward-looking role reassignment for Coco: less implementer, more reviewer and
64	translator. Most settled framing of the system's operating mode to date.
65	
66	**"Still not trusting the system to build things, but I'd say it's pretty good at creating
67	draft implementations and iterating."**
68	— Present-tense capability assessment. Not aspirational — actual. A real shift from the
69	"impatient to get things running" framing of six days ago.
70	
71	## By Experiment
72	
73	### X006 — Guild Metaphor
74	
75	No direct metaphor questions this session. One data point: no mention of vocabulary
76	friction despite a full day of commission work. The friction from the last interview
77	(register tax, apparatus naming cracks) didn't surface today. Either it's less salient
78	when working in the operational layer, or it's genuinely background noise now.
79	
80	### X008 — Patron's Hands
81	
82	**Coco's role is self-reorganizing.** The anticipated "clean shift" — from interactive
83	implementation to pipeline dispatch with Coco as reviewer — is emerging as Sean's own
84	expectation, not externally prescribed. He's articulating the transition without
85	prompting. This is consistent with the H1 signal from the last interview: the tight loop
86	may be self-loosening.
87	
88	**The "draft implementations" framing.** "Pretty good at creating draft implementations
89	and iterating" positions autonomous agents as draft producers, not final-output producers.
90	Coco's role in the new model is to close the gap between draft and shippable. This is a
91	stable operating pattern, not a failing one — but it's also not the eventual target state
92	(self-directed improvement loops, system evaluating its own outputs without Coco routing).
93	
94	**Partial outcomes as tech debt accelerators.** The concern about accumulated cruft from
95	repeated iteration is a new signal for X008 H3 (seeing vs. judging). When you only see
96	outputs, you can't observe whether the iteration path produced clean or messy intermediate
97	states. The suspicion that "more iterations = more debt" may become a structuring concern
98	for how Sean evaluates commission quality — not just "did it succeed" but "how many times
99	did it have to try."
100	
101	### X013 — Commission Outcomes
102	
103	**Normalize-IDs partial: framing trap identified.** The spec had Clerk in the table; the
104	framing "make everything work like Clerk" gave the anima logical cover to skip Clerk
105	changes. The information was present; the implication wasn't explicit enough. This is a
106	specific failure mode that's hard to prescribe against: not missing information, but
107	wording that licenses an incomplete reading.
108	
109	**The ceiling on patron craft.** Sean's explicit statement that he can't reliably catch
110	subtle implication traps at spec-writing time puts a ceiling on how much "better specs"
111	can improve outcomes. This is significant for H1: spec quality is a predictor, but there's
112	a floor of spec quality that humans can't reliably exceed. Planning and review cycles are
113	the structural answer — build a step that catches what the patron misses.
114	
115	**Abandoned outcome reframed as preferable.** The normalize-IDs first dispatch (abandoned,
116	no commit) is now explicitly better than a bad partial landing in the codebase. This is a
117	useful calibration: not all non-successes are equal; abandoned-clean may be preferable to
118	partial-messy. Worth tracking whether this holds as the commission volume grows.
119	
120	**Quality scorer: trusted, calibrated, slightly raises the floor.** Three properties: (1)
121	internally consistent across runs, (2) tracks patron assessment closely, (3) occasionally
122	flags things the patron would have let slide. This is the right profile for a useful
123	instrument — not rubber-stamping patron reads, but not diverging so far as to erode trust.
124	
125	**50% revision rate baseline confirmed emotionally.** The perception that it was "most"
126	when it was 50% is worth noting. Partial outcomes carry more weight than the raw number
127	suggests. Revision rate as a health indicator (H3) may understate the patron's subjective
128	experience of system quality.
129	
130	## Cross-Cutting Observations
131	
132	**The structural fix is a pipeline, not a practice.** Every thread in this interview leads
133	to the same answer: planning and review cycles as built-in pipeline steps, not patron
134	discipline or spec templates. The quality analysis prescription ("be exhaustive") is
135	already being resisted; the sustainable version has to be architectural.
136	
137	**Present-tense capability assessment, not aspiration.** "Pretty good at creating draft
138	implementations and iterating" is the first time Sean has described the system's actual
139	capability in settled, present-tense terms. Previous sessions have been forward-looking
140	("impatient," "hoping"). Something has shifted in confidence — probably the quality
141	analysis making the system's behavior legible.
142	
143	**The moral concern as background noise.** The AI ethics tension is present and
144	periodically surfaces, but it doesn't shape day-to-day decisions. Worth noting as a
145	standing condition rather than a variable.
146	
147	## Themes
148	
149	- `commission-writing` — framing traps; ceiling on patron craft; exhaustive spec as Coco
150	  compensation, not patron intention
151	- `tight-loop` — clean shift emerging: Coco as reviewer, not implementer
152	- `transition-to-autonomous` — "draft implementations" framing; stable operating pattern
153	  emerging
154	- `seeing-vs-judging` — partial outcomes land heavier; tech debt accumulation suspicion;
155	  quality scorer as calibration aid
156	- `frustration` — partial outcomes carry disproportionate weight; 50% felt like "most"
157	- `bootstrap-gap` — Coco's role reorganizing from implementer to reviewer+translator
158	- `engagement` — more settled than previous sessions; present-tense capability assessment
159	- `metaphor-fit` — no friction surfaced; vocabulary not salient in operational mode
160	- `commission-outcomes` — normalize-IDs framing trap; abandoned-clean vs partial-messy
161	  distinction; quality scorer profile
162	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1	# Interview — 2026-04-03
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Eleventh interview. Two days of dense activity since the last interview (2026-04-02T143340):
6	Walker Increment 3 landed and was reviewed, the Spider rename commission ran and was
7	reviewed, the web dashboard commission was reviewed, and the Fabricator pair was scored.
8	Several sessions focused on spec/design work for the Walker, Fabricator, and givens/yields
9	terminology. Coco continued heavy implementation and doc work throughout. Session notes
10	span 2026-04-02 afternoon through 2026-04-03 midday.
11	
12	## Key Quotes
13	
14	**"That wasn't what happened. Right off the bat, it didn't even load the page (just a
15	spinner). A couple rounds of fixes and Coco patched it up, but the functionality was
16	underwhelming... I'll be scrapping the whole thing."**
17	— On reviewing the web dashboard commission. Prior success in an earlier project set
18	expectations higher than the weak spec warranted. The broken first launch colored the
19	entire evaluation.
20

# Interview — 2026-04-03

## Context

Eleventh interview. Two days of dense activity since the last interview (2026-04-02T143340):
Walker Increment 3 landed and was reviewed, the Spider rename commission ran and was
reviewed, the web dashboard commission was reviewed, and the Fabricator pair was scored.
Several sessions focused on spec/design work for the Walker, Fabricator, and givens/yields
terminology. Coco continued heavy implementation and doc work throughout. Session notes
span 2026-04-02 afternoon through 2026-04-03 midday.

## Key Quotes

**"That wasn't what happened. Right off the bat, it didn't even load the page (just a
spinner). A couple rounds of fixes and Coco patched it up, but the functionality was
underwhelming... I'll be scrapping the whole thing."**
— On reviewing the web dashboard commission. Prior success in an earlier project set
expectations higher than the weak spec warranted. The broken first launch colored the
entire evaluation.

**"Probably both. That was my hope. Even if it didn't, we'd still get useful X013 data and
it only took me a minute or so to write the spec. So no harm, other than the emotional
letdown."**
— On whether he was betting on the agent filling spec gaps the same way a prior one did.
Clean articulation of the deliberate weak-spec dispatch as a low-cost data collection bet.

**"If I had done a review manually I would have seen it, yeah. Not sure if I would be
reviewing anima code and how much if it were all manual though."**
— On whether he would have caught the engine.id === 'review' hardcoding without the
quality scorer and Coco surfacing it. Direct admission that the review layers are enabling
evaluation depth he might not sustain without them.

**"I knew I didn't love 'walker' and am trying, in general, to rename apparatuses to feel
more like huge mechanical installations instead of just 'rooms' or 'roles with a capital
letter'. Spider hit the right note."**
— Clearest articulation yet of the naming register principle for apparatuses.

**"No, Parlour is definitely off. We'd accumulated a number of them — 'Parlour', 'Stacks',
'Scriptorium' — that are just places. Or ones that could even be just people — 'Clerk',
'Curator'. I don't love either set... Spider moves the needle back in the right direction
slightly."**
— On how many existing apparatus names fail the register. Knows the problem, living with
the debt.

**"Probably both. But definitely irritating."**
— On whether the naming debt is actively irritating or tolerable. Short and unambiguous.

**"If they also create the spec, that becomes a meaningless test. So, for now, Coco and I
own the docs."**
— On why documentation stays human-controlled. Not a trust issue — a methodological
principle. Docs are the control condition; if animas write the benchmark, the benchmark
loses validity.

## By Experiment

### X006 — Guild Metaphor

**"Huge mechanical installations" as the naming register principle.** For the first time,
Sean articulated a positive principle for apparatus naming — not just "this feels wrong"
but "this is what right looks like." The register is: giant, convoluted, mechanised things.
Walker was off because it sounds like a role or a motion. Spider works because it implies
a complex mechanical presence with reach and articulation.

**The apparatus naming fracture is significant and acknowledged.** Parlour, Stacks,
Scriptorium = places. Clerk, Curator = people. Neither category fits the principle. He
counts them — "the majority of apparatus names." Spider moves one in the right direction.
The principle exists; the vocabulary doesn't match it yet.

**The irritation is real.** "Definitely irritating" — not tolerated with equanimity but
with deliberate patience. The experimental discipline (don't rename until metaphor
experiments run) is creating a mild ongoing friction. Worth noting whether this changes
the baseline energy once cleanup is permitted.

### X008 — Patron's Hands

**The review layers as process visibility proxy.** The quality scorer and Coco together
surfaced the Walker Increment 3 architectural debt that Sean says he "probably wouldn't
have reviewed manually" with the same depth. This is the middle path pattern from the
last interview crystallizing further: not real-time visibility, not pure output judgment —
a structured post-hoc review layer that enables evaluation depth beyond what Sean would
sustain alone.

**"Not sure if I would be reviewing anima code and how much if it were all manual."** This
is a significant admission. The scorer isn't just confirming Sean's reads — it's enabling
a review discipline he might not maintain without it. H3 (seeing vs judging) may have a
third path: structured post-hoc review channels that give calibration without real-time
visibility.

**Documentation ownership as methodological control, not just trust.** "If they also
create the spec, that becomes a meaningless test." Sean and Coco owning the docs is a
principled experimental design decision: preserve the docs as an independent evaluation
baseline against which anima work can be measured. This will change when the system has
adversarial quality roles, but for now it's the control condition.

### X013 — Commission Outcomes

**Web dashboard (w-mni87qen) probed.** Complexity 20, weak spec, partial/broken. Key
findings:
- Sean dispatched with full knowledge the spec was weak — betting on agent UX sensibilities
  based on a prior success in an earlier project (likely Mk 2.0)
- The broken first launch (page didn't load) set a negative frame that colored the entire
  evaluation — even if subsequent functionality had been better
- The prior success was a meaningful expectation-setter; this is the "prior commission
  history shapes evaluation" pattern
- He'll scrap the whole thing — stronger than "partial" implies. Worth noting the
  mismatch between the log rating and the actual disposition

**Walker Increment 3 (w-mnif2seu) probed.** Complexity 5, strong spec, partial/incomplete.
Key findings:
- The engine.id === 'review' architectural debt was surfaced by the quality scorer and
  Coco, not by Sean examining the code directly
- Without those signals, he says he probably would have approved it on the functional
  "fruits" alone
- This directly instantiates the X008 H3 question in a concrete case: with process
  visibility layers, he caught structural debt; without them, the same code would have
  passed

## Cross-Cutting Observations

**First-load failure as evaluation primer.** The dashboard's broken first launch is a
practical finding about commission evaluation: a broken initial experience primes negative
reading of everything that follows. "Probably a lesson for demos that work in there
somewhere" — Sean connecting it to presentation contexts, but it applies equally to
commission review. The order of discovery shapes the judgment.

**Review infrastructure enabling evaluation discipline.** The scorer + Coco review layer
is not just a quality signal — it's enabling a review depth Sean wouldn't sustain manually.
This has a design implication: evaluation quality correlates with infrastructure quality,
not just patron discipline. This is a running theme emerging across X008 and X013.

**Documentation as experimental control.** "Coco and I own the docs" because docs are the
benchmark. The reasoning is sophisticated: it's not "I don't trust them" but "if they write
the criteria and meet the criteria, the test is circular." Sean is thinking about the
research validity of his own system. This may also foreshadow the structural design for
when docs can be commissioned: once adversarial quality roles exist that can evaluate doc
accuracy independently.

**Vocabulary irritation is accumulating.** "Definitely irritating" on apparatus names.
This is not new — it was noted in the last interview. But it's not fading with time; it's
steady background friction. When cleanup becomes available, it may happen quickly.

## Themes

- `commission-writing` — deliberate weak-spec dispatch as low-cost data bet; doc ownership
  as control condition
- `seeing-vs-judging` — scorer + Coco as structured post-hoc review layer; evaluation
  depth enabled by infrastructure; first-load failure as evaluation primer
- `metaphor-friction` — apparatus naming irritation steady; "huge mechanical installations"
  principle articulated but most names don't meet it
- `vocabulary-adoption` — Spider as the naming register correction; places/people vs
  mechanical installations taxonomy
- `bootstrap-gap` — doc ownership principled, not just trust-based; adversarial roles as
  future unlock
- `commission-outcomes` — prior success (earlier project) shaped dashboard expectations;
  broken launch colors evaluation; architectural debt surfaced by review layers not patron
- `engagement` — irritation at naming debt is steady; review infrastructure enabling
  evaluation depth; system becoming more legible
- `frustration` — dashboard emotional letdown; apparatus naming debt

content.content:
1	# Session Summary
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- Created `/workspace/guild-monitor/bin/wait-for-publish.sh` — polls for the `publish.yml` run on HEAD, streams it to completion, fetches remote git tags to derive the published version, outputs bare version to stdout with all status on stderr (so callers can cleanly capture it via `$()`)
6	- Created `/workspace/nexus/bin/wait-for-publish.sh` — identical pattern, scoped to the nexus repo
7	- Created `/workspace/nexus/bin/upgrade-dashboard.sh` — full pipeline: waits for guild-monitor publish → bumps `@shardworks/guild-monitor` in `packages/cli/package.json` → runs `pnpm install` to update lock file → commits + pushes → delegates to `upgrade-guild.sh`
8	- Rewrote `/workspace/nexus/bin/upgrade-guild.sh` to delegate the wait logic to `wait-for-publish.sh`, corrected package name from `@shardworks/nexus-core` → `@shardworks/nexus`, and replaced the full cache nuke + reinstall pattern with `npm uninstall -g` + `npm install -g @VERSION --prefer-online`
9	
10	## Decisions made and/or deferred
11	
12	- **`--prefer-online` instead of `npm cache clean --force`** — npm v5+ is content-addressed; there's no per-package cache clean. `--prefer-online` forces a fresh registry lookup for that specific install without trashing the whole cache.
13	- **Version pinned to exact tag, not `@latest`** — the install uses the version captured from the publish pipeline, not an open-ended `@latest`, so the installed version is guaranteed to match what was just published.
14	- **`@shardworks/nexus-core` corrected to `@shardworks/nexus`** — spotted as a stale reference while rewriting; fixed in passing.
15	
16	## Next steps & open questions
17	
18	- Scripts haven't been exercised end-to-end yet — worth a live run when the next guild-monitor publish happens.
19	- No consideration yet for what happens if `wait-for-publish.sh` is called and HEAD already has an *older* successful run (e.g. re-running the script after a failed upgrade). Currently it would find and re-watch the old run. Could be a problem or a feature, depending on intent.
20	
21	### Notable moments
22	
23	- Sean explicitly named `@shardworks/nexus` in the request ("the global nsg instance (@shardworks/nexus)") — which surfaced the stale `@shardworks/nexus-core` reference in the original script. The correction happened naturally without needing a separate call-out.
24	
25	### Experiment observations
26	
27	- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco did all implementation work this session (4 files created/modified). Sean directed the work; no commission was considered as an alternative. Task was well-scoped scripting work, a natural fit for direct implementation.
28	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

- Created `/workspace/nexus/bin/wait-for-publish.sh` — identical pattern, scoped to the nexus repo
- Created `/workspace/nexus/bin/upgrade-dashboard.sh` — full pipeline: waits for guild-monitor publish → bumps `@shardworks/guild-monitor` in `packages/cli/package.json` → runs `pnpm install` to update lock file → commits + pushes → delegates to `upgrade-guild.sh`

- No consideration yet for what happens if `wait-for-publish.sh` is called and HEAD already has an *older* successful run (e.g. re-running the script after a failed upgrade). Currently it would find and re-watch the old run. Could be a problem or a feature, depending on intent.

### Notable moments

1	# Session Summary — 2026-03-27T200000
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- **Reviewed ethnographer's X013 draft** — pulled it apart: identified contamination problem (spec quality and complexity both rated retrospectively), missing fourth failure cause (patron requirement error), H4 entanglement concern, rolling window noise, no reference class for revision rate.
6	- **Designed commission log** — split annotation into pre-dispatch (patron-filled, contamination-safe) and post-dispatch (system-populated + patron-filled). Fibonacci complexity scale (1/2/3/5/8/13/21) replacing simple/moderate/complex. Progressive automation design: manual → partial (patron assessment + writ relationships) → parallel signals (spec scorer anima).

1	# Session Summary — 2026-03-28T020849
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- Created `packages/rig` (`@shardworks/nexus-rig`) — guild runtime with `createRig()`, `Rig` interface, `NexusPlugin`/`NexusTool` types, `derivePluginKey()`, lazy cached plugin loading
6	- Added `ToolChannel` (`'cli' | 'mcp'`) and `allowedContexts` to `ToolDefinition` in core
7	- Restructured `packages/cli` — existing CLI to `src/v1/` as `nsg1`, new rig-powered `nsg` with dynamic tool registration, auto-nesting groups, boolean flag detection, Zod-to-Commander options
8	- Built rig built-in tools: `init`, `version`, `status`, `upgrade` (stub), `plugin list/install/remove/upgrade`
9	- Implemented real `nsg plugin install/remove` — npm install, tool discovery from exports, guild.json `plugins[]`, dependency checking via `nexus-plugin.json`
10	- Implemented `nsg init` — minimal guild bootstrap (guild.json, package.json, .gitignore, dirs)
11	- Added `BooksDatabase` interface to core with `execute(sql, args)` + `SqlResult`
12	- Created SQLite adapter in rig, injected into tool handler context via `Rig.getDatabase()`
13	- Added `PluginDescriptor` type to core (`nexus-plugin.json` shape with dependencies)
14	- MCP server: filters by `allowedContexts`, injects `db` into context
15	- Fixed v1 test path; 36 tests across rig (10) and cli (26), all passing
16	- Updated commission specs for `createRig()` model
17	
18	## Decisions made
19	
20	- `createRig()` sync, plugin loading lazy/cached
21	- Plugin key: `@shardworks/` stripped, other scopes drop `@` only
22	- Rig built-ins are regular tools alongside plugin tools
23	- CLI auto-nests only prefixes with 2+ tools
24	- Dropped workshop/tarball source types for plugin install
25	- `db` optional on `ToolContext` for backward compat
26	
27	## Open questions / next steps
28	
29	- **Book/Ledger abstraction** — strong direction: single "Book" primitive with JSON payload, expression indexes, optional immutability flag. No migrations. Could replace ALL data (plugins AND framework). Not yet implemented.
30	- Build Book abstraction → Assessment plugin as first consumer
31	- Eventually migrate core writs/sessions/events to books
32	
33	## Notable moments
34	
35	- Sean proposed replacing arbitrary SQL + migrations with declarative document stores ("what if we didn't provide an arbitrary SQL interface"). Eliminates the hardest part of plugin systems.
36	- Extended to entire framework: "is there a reason even writs and everything can't just be nosql-style books" — questions whether relational schema earns its keep at all.
37	- "we can model them as tools inside rig" — collapsed framework/plugin command distinction.
38	
39	## Experiment observations
40	
41	- **X008:** Extensive direct implementation by Coco across 6 packages. All work directed by Sean interactively, no commissions dispatched. Longest sustained Coco implementation session to date.
42

# Session Summary — 2026-03-28T020849

## What we did

- Created `packages/rig` (`@shardworks/nexus-rig`) — guild runtime with `createRig()`, `Rig` interface, `NexusPlugin`/`NexusTool` types, `derivePluginKey()`, lazy cached plugin loading
- Added `ToolChannel` (`'cli' | 'mcp'`) and `allowedContexts` to `ToolDefinition` in core
- Restructured `packages/cli` — existing CLI to `src/v1/` as `nsg1`, new rig-powered `nsg` with dynamic tool registration, auto-nesting groups, boolean flag detection, Zod-to-Commander options
- Built rig built-in tools: `init`, `version`, `status`, `upgrade` (stub), `plugin list/install/remove/upgrade`
- Implemented real `nsg plugin install/remove` — npm install, tool discovery from exports, guild.json `plugins[]`, dependency checking via `nexus-plugin.json`
- Implemented `nsg init` — minimal guild bootstrap (guild.json, package.json, .gitignore, dirs)
- Added `BooksDatabase` interface to core with `execute(sql, args)` + `SqlResult`
- Created SQLite adapter in rig, injected into tool handler context via `Rig.getDatabase()`
- Added `PluginDescriptor` type to core (`nexus-plugin.json` shape with dependencies)
- MCP server: filters by `allowedContexts`, injects `db` into context
- Fixed v1 test path; 36 tests across rig (10) and cli (26), all passing
- Updated commission specs for `createRig()` model

## Decisions made

- `createRig()` sync, plugin loading lazy/cached
- Plugin key: `@shardworks/` stripped, other scopes drop `@` only
- Rig built-ins are regular tools alongside plugin tools
- CLI auto-nests only prefixes with 2+ tools
- Dropped workshop/tarball source types for plugin install
- `db` optional on `ToolContext` for backward compat

## Open questions / next steps

- **Book/Ledger abstraction** — strong direction: single "Book" primitive with JSON payload, expression indexes, optional immutability flag. No migrations. Could replace ALL data (plugins AND framework). Not yet implemented.
- Build Book abstraction → Assessment plugin as first consumer
- Eventually migrate core writs/sessions/events to books

## Notable moments

- Sean proposed replacing arbitrary SQL + migrations with declarative document stores ("what if we didn't provide an arbitrary SQL interface"). Eliminates the hardest part of plugin systems.
- Extended to entire framework: "is there a reason even writs and everything can't just be nosql-style books" — questions whether relational schema earns its keep at all.
- "we can model them as tools inside rig" — collapsed framework/plugin command distinction.

## Experiment observations

- **X008:** Extensive direct implementation by Coco across 6 packages. All work directed by Sean interactively, no commissions dispatched. Longest sustained Coco implementation session to date.

1	# Session Summary
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- **Reviewed Walker Increment 3 dispatch** (`w-mnif2seu-e879825d08eb`, commit `3b4ac6a`). Single commit, 714 lines, 39 tests passing. Quality scores: blind 2.75, aware 2.40. Outcome: success, no revision required.
6	- **Identified hardcoded `engine.id === 'review'` branch** in Spider's `tryCollect` as a structural smell. All six quality scorer runs flagged it.
7	- **Designed the engine collect callback** — optional `collect?(sessionId, givens, context)` on `EngineDesign`. Iterated through several signatures before settling on `(sessionId, givens, context: EngineRunContext)` — sessionId prominent as primary input, givens and context mirror `run()` for API consistency.
8	- **Designed `AnimateHandle.sessionId`** — exposed session ID on the handle immediately so quick engines can return without awaiting session completion. Identified that engines were blocking on `await handle.result` just to extract the sessionId, defeating the launch/collect split.
9	- **Updated three apparatus specs** (animator.md, fabricator.md, spider.md) with both changes.
10	- **Implemented all code changes** across 4 packages, 11 files. Framework commits: `6e16fde` (feat), `fdb3043` (docs). Pushed to origin/main.
11	- **Discussed run-blocking concern** — Spider `await`s `design.run()`, so slow engines block the crawl loop. Decided to leave for now; the Executor apparatus must solve this with non-blocking execution and persisted yields. Captured in Spider spec's "not covered" section.
12	
13	## Decisions made and/or deferred
14	
15	- **`collect` signature: option B** — `(sessionId, givens, context: EngineRunContext)`. Reuses existing type, no new `EngineCollectContext` needed.
16	- **`AnimateRequest.sessionId`** — clean optional field, not a private `_sessionId` hack. Lets `summon()` pre-generate and thread through to `animate()`.
17	- **Spider run-blocking: deferred to Executor.** Acceptable for single-process MVP. Executor must persist yields to a book for remote/Docker runners.
18	- **Walker renamed to Spider** — ongoing commission landed during session; all work used new names.
19	
20	## Next steps & open questions
21	
22	- Commission log entry for `w-mnif2seu-e879825d08eb` still needs `spec_quality_pre` and outcome fields filled in
23	- `.scratch/specs/engine-collect-callback.md` is now stale (work implemented directly) — clean up or delete
24	- The `engine-collect-callback` commission spec was never dispatched — the work was done by Coco directly
25	
26	### Notable moments
27	
28	- **"is it possible for the engine to have its own sessionrecord internally? why does it need to get passed in like that?"** — Sean pushed back on passing `SessionRecord` to `collect`, leading to the realization that engines pull their own deps via `guild()` and just need the sessionId.
29	- **"how is run implemented if it doesnt even start a session?"** — Sean's question exposed that engines DO start sessions and await completion in `run()`, meaning the launch/collect split was ceremonial. Led to the `AnimateHandle.sessionId` fix.
30	- **"since it's an extension point we probably want to harden against a misbehaving plugin blocking the system"** — Identified that `await design.run()` is a systemic vulnerability. Led to the Executor design constraint: non-blocking execution with persisted yields, essential for remote runners.
31	
32	### Experiment observations
33	
34	- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco implemented the engine collect callback and AnimateHandle.sessionId directly — 11 files, ~230 lines changed across 4 packages. Sean directed architecture and API design; Coco wrote all code. No commission was dispatched. This is the largest implementation Coco has done directly, though the changes were mechanical (interface additions, moving existing logic, updating call sites).
35	- **X013 (Commission Outcomes):** Walker Increment 3 reviewed — complexity 5, strong spec, success outcome. Quality: blind 2.75, aware 2.40. Aware mode dinged `code_structure` (2 vs 3) and `requirement_coverage` (2) for the hardcoded collect branch and missing reviewer role in diff.
36

- **Discussed run-blocking concern** — Spider `await`s `design.run()`, so slow engines block the crawl loop. Decided to leave for now; the Executor apparatus must solve this with non-blocking execution and persisted yields. Captured in Spider spec's "not covered" section.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **`collect` signature: option B** — `(sessionId, givens, context: EngineRunContext)`. Reuses existing type, no new `EngineCollectContext` needed.
- **`AnimateRequest.sessionId`** — clean optional field, not a private `_sessionId` hack. Lets `summon()` pre-generate and thread through to `animate()`.
- **Spider run-blocking: deferred to Executor.** Acceptable for single-process MVP. Executor must persist yields to a book for remote/Docker runners.
- **Walker renamed to Spider** — ongoing commission landed during session; all work used new names.

## Next steps & open questions

- Commission log entry for `w-mnif2seu-e879825d08eb` still needs `spec_quality_pre` and outcome fields filled in
- `.scratch/specs/engine-collect-callback.md` is now stale (work implemented directly) — clean up or delete
- The `engine-collect-callback` commission spec was never dispatched — the work was done by Coco directly

### Notable moments

- **"is it possible for the engine to have its own sessionrecord internally? why does it need to get passed in like that?"** — Sean pushed back on passing `SessionRecord` to `collect`, leading to the realization that engines pull their own deps via `guild()` and just need the sessionId.
- **"how is run implemented if it doesnt even start a session?"** — Sean's question exposed that engines DO start sessions and await completion in `run()`, meaning the launch/collect split was ceremonial. Led to the `AnimateHandle.sessionId` fix.
- **"since it's an extension point we probably want to harden against a misbehaving plugin blocking the system"** — Identified that `await design.run()` is a systemic vulnerability. Led to the Executor design constraint: non-blocking execution with persisted yields, essential for remote runners.

### Experiment observations

- **X008 (Patron's Hands):** Coco implemented the engine collect callback and AnimateHandle.sessionId directly — 11 files, ~230 lines changed across 4 packages. Sean directed architecture and API design; Coco wrote all code. No commission was dispatched. This is the largest implementation Coco has done directly, though the changes were mechanical (interface additions, moving existing logic, updating call sites).
- **X013 (Commission Outcomes):** Walker Increment 3 reviewed — complexity 5, strong spec, success outcome. Quality: blind 2.75, aware 2.40. Aware mode dinged `code_structure` (2 vs 3) and `requirement_coverage` (2) for the hardcoded collect branch and missing reviewer role in diff.

input.content:
# X008 — The Patron's Hands: Findings & Analysis

*Compiled 2026-04-03 from 11 ethnographer interviews (2026-03-22 through 2026-04-03), 90+ session notes, commission log data, and spec observations.*

---

## Overview

X008 asks what happens when an interactive agent fills the autonomous agent role during a
bootstrap period, and how that shapes the patron's expectations and behavior when autonomous
dispatch comes online. The experiment was designed around a specific structural fact: the
Nexus Mk 2.1 system was built largely through Coco (the interactive collaborator) before any
autonomous agents were commissioned. Coco removed version slots across 24 files, renamed
"implement" → "tool" across 57 files, built the Stacks apparatus (~2,200 lines), the Animator
(~1,500 lines), the Parlour (~1,200 lines), the full plugin/apparatus architecture, and dozens
of mechanical fixes and upgrades. All of this was patron-directed interactive work — a tight
collaborative loop — before a single commission was dispatched to an autonomous anima.

The question was whether that baseline experience would become a trap.

---

## Hypothesis Assessments

---

### H1 — The Tight Loop Trap

**Hypothesis:** Interactive bootstrap creates expectations that make the transition to autonomous
agents feel like a downgrade — even if output quality is equivalent.

**Verdict: Partially confirmed — but with a critical inversion.**

The tight loop did create a baseline, and that baseline did shape expectations. But the
dominant effect was not the one predicted. Rather than creating *attachment to the loop*, the
loop made the costs of the loop visible once an alternative appeared.

The clearest statement came from the first post-dispatch session (2026-03-25):

> *"With Coco, I give some instructions and know there is going to be a follow-up: perhaps
> more changes, or at a minimum instructions to wrap-up and push everything. So I'm never
> really 'free' even if I'm not physically typing the code. With a process that's more opaque,
> I might unlock more of that freedom."*

The loss of real-time steering was framed not as deprivation but as potential liberation. The
tight loop's costs — never quite off the hook, always a follow-up pending — were invisible
until the contrast of autonomous dispatch made them legible.

A month later (2026-04-02), Sean was articulating a "clean shift" spontaneously:

> *"I think there will be a pretty clean shift from 'have Coco implement things interactively'
> to 'dispatch, collect experiment data via pipeline, and have Coco review+suggest followups'."*

This was his own framing, not a response to the experiment's prompting. The transition was
self-organizing and felt like forward progress, not loss.

**Where H1 does hold:** The tight loop created a *fear-based* dispatch heuristic that
persisted well after autonomous dispatch became available. From 2026-03-27:

> *"It's intuitive... fear-based, maybe. I get an anxiety signal if something feels dangerous
> to dispatch blindly."*

This heuristic — core/critical work to Coco, new/self-contained work to commissions — wasn't
rationally derived. It was an emotional residue of having watched implementation happen in
real time and knowing what the high-risk paths felt like. The tight loop didn't create
attachment; it calibrated caution.

**What H1 missed:** The hypothesis assumed the patron would *prefer* watching work happen.
What actually happened: the patron was *comfortable* watching during the bootstrap, and that
comfort masked how much the constant follow-up cadence cost. The transition didn't feel like
a downgrade because the patron was already impatient for something else:

> *"It feels 'expedient'. But I'm impatient for getting this thing off the ground, running
> autonomously."* (2026-04-02)

The "trap" wasn't attachment to the tight loop. It was the slow realization that the tight loop
was itself the delay.

**Design implication:** The H1 prediction ("explicit transition protocol needed") may still
be useful — not because patrons will resist the transition, but because the transition is
happening unevenly. The fear-based dispatch heuristic persists even when it could safely be
loosened. Explicit calibration checkpoints ("would this task actually break anything if an
agent got it wrong?") may accelerate the heuristic's update rate.

---

### H2 — Conversational Implementation Follows a Different Shape

**Hypothesis:** Interactive implementation produces smaller, more incremental changes with
tighter scope; commission-based implementation produces larger, self-contained chunks. These
are structurally different work patterns.

**Verdict: Confirmed — but the organizing principle is more nuanced than predicted.**

The split between Coco work and commission work is observable and consistent across the full
dataset. Session notes show the pattern clearly:

- **Coco work:** Cross-cutting renames (57 files), version slot removals, mechanical fixes
  from code reviews, CI hardening, upgrade scripts, documentation updates, operational
  scaffolding. Typically: a clear target state, mechanical execution, tight feedback at
  decision points.

- **Commission work:** Full apparatus implementations (Clerk, Dispatch, Scriptorium, Animator
  variants), new tool features, self-contained additions. Typically: a bounded scope, a spec
  that describes expected behavior, output evaluated after the fact.

The split became an explicit heuristic by 2026-03-27:

> *"Anything that is core/critical enough that it could break the framework I still do manually
> with Coco. Everything else, I try to commission. Especially if it's new or self-contained."*

**What the hypothesis got right:** Commission work does tend to be larger and more
self-contained. The granularity difference is real. A typical Coco task is 1–3 files and 20–200
lines; a typical commission is 5–15 files and 500–1500 lines, with a spec and verification
criteria.

**What the hypothesis underspecified:** The primary organizing principle is *risk*, not task
size or type. The heuristic is fear-based: will getting this wrong block me? Small tasks can go
to commissions (the vocabulary rename spec was a one-off), and large tasks can stay with Coco
(the engine collect callback: 11 files, ~230 lines, implemented directly on 2026-04-03). The
key variable is perceived downside, not scope.

This reframes the design implication. H2 suggested we might need a different dispatch pattern
for "naturally conversational-sized" work (micro-commissions, standing animas). The more useful
framing: the pattern that's emerging is a *risk-stratified* dispatch model. Coco absorbs the
work where getting it wrong is consequential or where the patron needs real-time steering to
navigate uncertainty. Commissions absorb the work where failure is recoverable and scope is
clear.

A second nuance: Coco's role in the commission pipeline is not separate from autonomous dispatch
— it's part of it. Coco drafts commission specs, reviews outputs, interprets results for the
patron, and implements review fixes. This means commission granularity includes the wrapper
work that Coco does around autonomous sessions, not just the sessions themselves.

**The "who is right for this kind of reasoning?" dimension:** A pattern noted in the spec
observations (2026-03-27) that wasn't in the original hypothesis: Coco drafts commission specs
as part of the design conversation, then Sean dispatches them. Coco is doing patron-adjacent
reasoning (specifying what to build), not implementation. The H2 granularity question isn't
just "how big" but "which kind of cognition is needed" — and some cognition (architectural
decision-making under uncertainty, spec authorship) is naturally interactive even when the
execution isn't.

---

### H3 — Seeing the Work Changes the Judgment

**Hypothesis:** Watching implementation happen makes the patron more forgiving of imperfection
(understands tradeoffs, forgives reasonable compromises). Judging only output makes the patron
harsher and less calibrated.

**Verdict: Partially confirmed — but the data reveals a third path that the binary framing
missed.**

**Where H3 holds:** The in-the-moment vs. retrospective response to failures does differ. When
Ergon committed to the wrong branch (2026-03-27), the immediate reaction was frustration that
eroded confidence — *"Every time something like that happens I lose a bit of confidence in the
system."* The equanimity ("the whole thing can just be redone") emerged in retrospect, not in
the moment. This is consistent with H3's prediction: pure output evaluation triggers more
immediate negative reactions.

The quality scorer data also supports H3's logic. Without the scorer and Coco's review, the
Walker Increment 3 architectural debt (hardcoded `engine.id === 'review'` branch) would likely
have been approved on the functional "fruits" alone:

> *"If I had done a review manually I would have seen it, yeah. Not sure if I would be
> reviewing anima code and how much if it were all manual though."* (2026-04-03)

Judgment without structured process visibility does appear to be shallower and potentially
miscalibrated.

**Where H3 is complicated:** The tight loop doesn't actually give the patron the code-level
visibility H3 assumed. From 2026-03-27:

> *"Even while working collaboratively with Coco I was only reviewing a fraction of the code."*

The safety differential between tight-loop and autonomous dispatch is smaller than it feels.
The patron is trusting Coco's execution in both cases — the difference is the presence of a
steering channel, not a verification channel. This deflates the H3 premise: the tight loop
wasn't providing genuine calibration through visibility; it was providing *conversational
confidence through dialogue*.

**The third path — structured post-hoc review:** What actually improved evaluation quality
was neither real-time visibility nor pure output evaluation, but a specific infrastructure:
quality scorer (automated) + Coco as post-hoc reviewer. From 2026-04-03:

> *"It's been internally consistent, and tracking my own assessments pretty closely. It's
> probably caught more things than I have, to be honest."* (on the quality scorer)

And:

> *"If I had done a review manually I would have seen it, yeah. Not sure if I would be
> reviewing anima code and how much if it were all manual though."*

The review infrastructure is not just confirming the patron's reads — it's enabling evaluation
depth that the patron would not sustain without structural support. This is a significant design
implication: the H3 prescription ("autonomous agents need to expose more reasoning") is right,
but the mechanism isn't process transparency (showing the work). It's a post-hoc review layer
that closes the gap between output evaluation and calibrated judgment.

**The commission output-as-draft pattern:** By 2026-04-02, autonomous outputs were being
framed as draft implementations:

> *"Still not trusting the system to build things, but I'd say it's pretty good at creating
> draft implementations and iterating."*

This framing resolves the H3 tension: outputs aren't judged as finished work against a clean
standard, but as inputs to a review cycle where gaps are identified and follow-up commissions
are dispatched. The "fruits" judgment is real, but it's one step in a pipeline rather than a
final verdict.

---

## Additional Findings (Beyond the Hypotheses)

### 1. The Bootstrap Period Built the Foundation, Not Just Expectations

X008 was designed to study how the bootstrap shaped the *patron's mental model*. It also
shaped the *codebase and architecture in ways that enabled autonomous dispatch*. The March 28 –
April 2 rebuild (all Coco-driven) was explicitly motivated by making autonomous dispatch safer
and more tractable:

> *"I'm hoping this buys better agent outcomes, because they work on self-contained pieces
> without breaking the whole system."*

The plugin/apparatus architecture, the monorepo restructure, the clean package boundaries —
all built interactively — are what made the first autonomous commissions viable. The bootstrap
wasn't a liability whose effects need managing. It was construction work that created the
conditions for a different relationship with autonomous agents.

### 2. Cost-of-Effort Asymmetry as a Mental Model Lag

A real-time insight from 2026-03-27 that was noted explicitly but is not yet fully integrated:

> *"The interesting thing about that is it's sort of a relic of building things myself...
> Losing code I write is incredibly frustrating, and a big setback. But even if a whole
> commission is lost it doesn't really matter. There's maybe a token cost or whatever, but
> the whole thing can just be redone. That's an interesting thing to note, and something
> I'll need to internalize more."*

The patron's anxiety about agent failures is calibrated to the cost of *his own effort*, not
to token costs. A failed commission costs tokens, not hours. This miscalibration keeps the
dispatch anxiety floor higher than the actual risk warrants. Sean identified this as a holdover
from self-built code culture, named it explicitly, and said he needs to internalize it — but
as of April 3, it's still an intellectual insight rather than an embodied prior.

This may be the most durable finding from X008 for practitioners: the effort-cost asymmetry is
a specific, named mental model lag that affects dispatch confidence. It deserves explicit
surfacing and a calibration mechanism (e.g., tracking actual cost-to-redo of failed commissions
vs. the felt loss).

### 3. Coco's Role Migrated Rather Than Being Displaced

The hypothesis framing positioned Coco and autonomous agents as alternatives during the
transition. What actually happened: Coco's role migrated. The trajectory:

- **Pre-autonomous (March 22 – March 25):** Coco as primary implementer, doing 100% of
  implementation work interactively.
- **Early autonomous (March 25 – March 27):** Coco retained for core/critical work; autonomous
  agents for new/self-contained work. Coco also reviews autonomous outputs via mediated code
  review.
- **Stable operating pattern (April 2):** Coco as spec author, reviewer, interpreter. Autonomous
  agents as draft producers. Coco no longer primarily an implementer — that role is now shared
  with autonomous agents, but the commission pipeline is the default for new self-contained work.
- **Current (April 3):** Coco still doing direct implementation for high-risk architectural
  work (the collect callback: 11 files, 230 lines), spec drafting, review, and follow-up fix
  implementation from review artifacts.

The migration is not complete. Coco remains an implementer for work that is either too risky
for autonomous dispatch or too tightly coupled to real-time architectural decisions. The
endpoint (self-directed improvement loops without Coco routing) is articulated and aspired to
but not reached.

Sean drew a hard line on the destination (2026-04-02):

> *"If it's a permanent part of the workflow I'd consider the system a failure."*

He means Coco-as-reviewer as a permanent oversight pattern — not Coco as interactive
collaborator. This is a calibrated ambition: Coco continues as a design and conversation
partner; what he wants to eliminate is Coco as a *quality bridge* that substitutes for the
system's own self-evaluation capacity.

### 4. The First Commission Result Was Mediated Before It Was Experienced

A detail from X007 that has X008 implications: the first autonomous commission result (the
guild monitor web dashboard) was reviewed by Coco before Sean saw it. Coco gave high praise.
Sean then saw it through that positive frame.

This became a pattern. By April 3, the pipeline is: autonomous commission → scorer →
Coco review → patron. Raw output evaluation is not the norm. Every output is pre-interpreted
before Sean encounters it.

This has two implications for H3: (a) the "seeing vs. judging" binary in H3 was already
resolved in practice — the system built a structured middle path rather than choosing a pole;
(b) the patron's evaluation disposition toward autonomous work is consistently mediated, which
means the real variable is the quality of the mediation layer, not the patron's raw
calibration.

---

## Synthesis: What Actually Happened

The bootstrap period produced three distinct effects that were not fully anticipated:

**1. It made the loop's costs visible.** The tight loop was the only mode available during
bootstrap, so its costs were invisible. The first autonomous commission made those costs
legible — and what the patron found was that he'd been less free than he realized. This is an
inversion of H1 but still a bootstrap effect: you can only recognize the loop's costs by
escaping it temporarily.

**2. It built the preconditions for trust — not trust itself.** The rebuild that happened
through Coco wasn't just implementation. It was the construction of the architecture (plugin
system, clean package boundaries, self-contained pieces) that makes autonomous dispatch safer.
The bootstrap period was the patron building the conditions he needed before he could extend
confidence to autonomous agents. The sequence was necessary, not wasteful.

**3. It shaped a middle path rather than a transition.** The X008 framing anticipated a
transition: from tight-loop to autonomous. What actually emerged was a stable hybrid where
different modes fill genuinely different roles. The tight loop didn't get replaced — it evolved
into a review and steering layer that surrounds, rather than substitutes for, autonomous work.

---

## Implications for System Design

**For multi-agent system builders:**

- **Plan for the Coco role, not just the agent role.** An interactive collaborator that can
  work alongside autonomous agents — for spec drafting, output review, architectural decisions,
  and follow-up implementation — is not a temporary bootstrap convenience. It's a permanent
  role in a mature multi-agent workflow. Design it explicitly.

- **The dispatch heuristic will be fear-based initially.** Patrons new to autonomous dispatch
  develop risk-based intuitions ("anxiety signals") rather than deliberated capability analyses.
  This is rational given limited history with agent behavior, but it also means the heuristic
  updates slowly. Build explicit calibration tools: tracked success rates by task type,
  cost-to-redo data, visible failure recovery paths.

- **Output mediation is not a workaround.** Structured post-hoc review (automated scoring +
  interpretive agent) enables evaluation depth that patrons won't sustain manually. This isn't
  compensating for patron limitations — it's a structural component of reliable evaluation. The
  alternative (raw output judgment) produces shallower reads and misses architectural debt.

- **Expose the effort-cost asymmetry explicitly.** Patrons from self-build backgrounds carry
  a calibrated dread of losing their own work that is wildly miscalibrated for agent work. A
  failed commission costs $2–4 and an hour of calendar time. Name this asymmetry early. Track
  and display actual cost-to-redo numbers. The miscalibration suppresses dispatch confidence
  more than actual risk warrants.

- **The bootstrap codebase is a baseline, not a liability.** Work done through the interactive
  agent during bootstrap shapes the codebase that autonomous agents will operate on. Investing
  in good architecture during the interactive phase pays dividends when autonomous dispatch
  begins — better package boundaries mean smaller blast radius, fewer cross-cutting failures,
  more recoverable mistakes.

**For patrons entering their first autonomous dispatch period:**

- The transition doesn't feel like a downgrade. It feels like getting time back. Expect this.

- Your anxiety about dispatching will be higher than the actual risk warrants. Track what
  actually goes wrong. The failure modes that matter most are recoverable ones with modest
  token cost — not catastrophic ones requiring manual intervention.

- Your first instinct will be to keep more work in the interactive loop because it feels safer.
  It is marginally safer, but at a cost (constant follow-up, never fully off the hook) that
  accumulates invisibly until you have an alternative.

- Build a review layer before you need it. The quality of your evaluation of autonomous output
  depends heavily on whether you have structured post-hoc review infrastructure. Without it,
  you'll approve work you'd catch with scoring and a review pass.

---

## Guidance for Future Architects of AI-Assisted Development Systems

**Design the three modes from the start, not one at a time:**
1. Interactive collaboration (patron + agent, real-time)
2. Autonomous dispatch (patron posts spec, agent completes, patron evaluates)
3. Post-hoc review (structured evaluation of autonomous output, possibly by a different agent)

Most systems are designed with only mode 2 in mind. Modes 1 and 3 are often treated as
workarounds. The data here suggests they are permanent and necessary.

**The tight loop is not a temporary scaffold.** It doesn't go away when agents are good enough.
It changes role: from primary production mode to oversight and steering layer. Design the
interaction contract for that mature role, not just for "we haven't built autonomous agents yet."

**Trust is accumulated through successful-failure cycles, not through capability thresholds.**
The patron's dispatch confidence grew not when the system became more capable, but when the
pattern of recoverable failures became familiar. Trust calibration is a learning process.
Design for evidence accumulation: visible success rates, trackable failure modes, legible
recovery paths.

**The patron's mental model is a lagging indicator.** The system's actual capability will
generally exceed the patron's willingness to dispatch. The bottleneck is not model capability
but patron confidence. Design for confidence calibration as much as for output quality.

---

## Ideas for Publishable Work

### 1. "The Tight Loop Tax: Hidden Costs of Interactive AI Collaboration" *(practitioner article)*

**Target:** software practitioners, AI engineering blogs (Increment, The Pragmatic Engineer)

The tight loop between a developer and an AI coding agent carries costs that are invisible
until you have an alternative: you're never fully off the hook, there's always a follow-up
pending, steering mid-implementation keeps you attending even when you don't need to. This
piece would quantify the productivity cost of interactive-as-default for work that could be
dispatched autonomously, and argue for deliberate mode-switching as a discipline.

---

### 2. "Bootstrapping a Multi-Agent System: What You Build Before You Can Build It" *(technical blog post)*

**Target:** AI engineering audiences, the HuggingFace/LangChain community

A case study of the bootstrap period in Nexus Mk 2.1: how the interactive phase shaped the
architecture that made autonomous dispatch viable, what the interactive agent built before
autonomous agents came online, and how the "wrong" phase (implementing through the wrong agent)
was actually foundation work. Would challenge the common advice to "start autonomous from day
one" — there's a legitimate bootstrap sequence, and understanding it prevents false starts.

---

### 3. "Who Reviews the Agents? Calibrated Evaluation in Multi-Agent Systems" *(research article or conference talk)*

**Target:** academic venues (CSCW, CHI) or applied ML conferences (MLSys, SysML)

**Core argument:** Pure output evaluation (judge only the fruits) is structurally insufficient
for complex multi-agent outputs. Real-time process visibility is also insufficient (patrons
don't actually review much of what interactive agents do). The middle path — structured
post-hoc review combining automated scoring with an interpretive agent — provides better
calibration than either extreme. This paper would present the X008/X013 data, characterize
the three evaluation modes, and offer design principles for the review layer.

**Testable claim:** Patrons who have structured post-hoc review infrastructure catch more
structural debt and have better-calibrated quality assessments than those relying on unaided
output evaluation.

---

### 4. "The Effort-Cost Asymmetry: Why Developers Underuse Autonomous AI Agents" *(short paper or workshop)*

**Target:** ICSE, SOUPS, or AI/UX workshops

**Core argument:** Developers from self-build backgrounds carry a fear of losing work that is
well-calibrated for their own effort (losing 8 hours of coding is a real cost) but wildly
miscalibrated for autonomous agent work (a failed commission costs $2-4 and can be re-run in
minutes). This asymmetry suppresses autonomous dispatch confidence in ways that are
identifiable, predictable, and potentially designable-against. This paper would characterize
the asymmetry, trace its source (self-build culture internalized), and propose interface
interventions (visible cost-to-redo tracking, "what's the worst case?" prompts).

---

### 5. "The Bootstrap Condition in Human-AI Teaming" *(conference talk or panel contribution)*

**Target:** NeurIPS workshop on human-AI interaction, ACL/EMNLP applied track

The bootstrap period in human-AI teaming — when the human uses the AI in a mode that won't
scale, because the scalable mode isn't ready — creates effects on expectations, habits, and
system design that persist into the operational phase. This talk would use X008 as a case
study to argue that the bootstrap condition is undertheorized in human-AI teaming literature:
it's treated as a transition rather than as a phase with its own design requirements. Would
call for explicit bootstrap-phase design: what does the patron do in this phase, what
expectations should they form, and what architectural investments pay off in the transition?

---

*Document status: Initial synthesis, as of 2026-04-03. X008 is still active — the transition
to autonomous dispatch is ongoing. Key open questions: Does the effort-cost asymmetry resolve
as commission volume grows? Does the fear-based dispatch heuristic update measurably? Does
Coco's role as reviewer eventually give way to system-native evaluation? These will inform
later revisions.*
